diagrams.title: Bayes' Theorem Probability Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: A visual representation showing how prior knowledge and observed data combine to produce posterior probability
elements: Rectangle node 'Prior P(H)' on the left - represents initial belief before observing data
Rectangle node 'Likelihood P(D|H)' - shows probability of data given hypothesis
Rectangle node 'Evidence P(D)' at bottom - total probability of observing the data
Rectangle node 'Posterior P(H|D)' on the right - updated belief after seeing data
Arrow from Prior to Posterior labeled 'P(H|D) = P(D|H) × P(H) / P(D)'
Arrow from Likelihood to Posterior
Arrow from Evidence to Poscoming from bottom
Curved arrow showing evidence as 'normalizing constant' that ensures posterior sums to 1
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to visualize Bayes' Theorem as a flow from prior knowledge through evidence to updated posterior belief, understanding how each component relates to the others mathematically and conceptually

diagrams.title: Bayesian Learning Cycle Process Flow
diagrams.description: Circular flowchart showing the iterative nature of Bayesian learning
elements: Oval at top labeled 'Initial Hypothesis (Prior)' - starting beliefs about the model
Arrow pointing clockwise to 'Observe Data D' - collecting training examples
Arrow to 'Calculate Likelihood P(D|H)' - how well hypothesis explains the data
Arrow to 'Update using Bayes Rule' - computing posterior from prior and likelihood
Arrow to 'New Hypothesis (Posterior)' - refined beliefs after learning
Arrow returning to top completing the cycle
Small text inside cycle: 'Repeat as new data arrives'
Dotted outer circle labeled 'Bayesian Learning Process'
diagrams.purpose: Students understand Bayesian learning as an iterative, continuous process where beliefs are continuously updated as new data is observed, reinforcing the dynamic nature of probabilistic learning

diagrams.title: Naïve Bayes Classifier Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the internal structure and decision process of a Naïve Bayes classifier
elements: Input layer: Rectangle 'Test Instance X = (x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)' with feature values
Arrow to 'Calculate Class Priors P(Y)' box - probability of each class from training data
Arrow to 'Calculate Likelihood P(Xᵢ|Y)' boxes - one for each feature, showing conditional probabilities
Arrows from each likelihood box to 'Apply Naïve Independence Assumption' combining them
Arrow to 'Calculate Posterior P(Y|X)' using Bayes theorem for each class
Diamond decision node 'Select Maximum Posterior' comparing all class posteriors
Output: Rectangle 'Predicted Class Y*' showing the predicted label
Side box showing formula: P(Y|X) ∝ P(Y) × ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ|Y)
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the complete flow through a Naïve Bayes classifier, understanding how the independence assumption simplifies calculations while seeing the step-by-step process from input to prediction

diagrams.title: Medical Diagnosis Example - Bayes Theorem Application
diagrams.description: Tree diagram illustrating the classic medical diagnosis example with disease and test results
elements: Root node 'Patient' splits into two branches
Left branch labeled 'Has Disease (D)' with probability P(D)=0.01 (1%)
Right branch labeled 'No Disease (¬D)' with probability P(¬D)=0.99 (99%)
Each branch splits again: Positive Test (+) and Negative Test (-)
Under 'Has Disease': P(+|D)=0.99 (99% sensitivity), P(-|D)=0.01
Under 'No Disease': P(+|¬D)=0.05 (5% false positive), P(-|¬D)=0.95
Leaf nodes show joint probabilities: P(D,+) = 0.01×0.99 = 0.0099
Bottom box 'Apply Bayes Rule: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / P(+)'
Annotation: 'Despite positive test, only 16.7% actually has disease!'
diagrams.purpose: Students see a concrete application of Bayes Theorem, understanding how base rate (prior probability) affects the interpretation of test results and learning to calculate posterior probabilities in real-world scenarios

diagrams.title: Bayesian vs Frequentist Approach Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side block diagram contrasting the two statistical paradigms
elements: Left column titled 'Frequentist Approach': Fixed parameter θ as unknown constant, Confidence intervals, P(data|θ), No prior distribution, Point estimates with bounds, Long-run frequency interpretation

Right column titled 'Bayesian Approach': Parameter θ as random variable with distribution, Probability intervals (credible intervals), P(θ|data), Prior distribution over parameters, Full posterior distribution, Degree of belief interpretation
Center divider showing 'Data' flowing into both approaches
Arrows from both to 'Estimates/Predictions' at bottom
Top labels: 'What is the probability that the parameter lies in a range?' - Frequentist: 'Not defined' | Bayesian: 'Direct probability from posterior'
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental philosophical differences between frequentist and Bayesian approaches, recognizing why Bayesian methods provide more intuitive probability statements about parameters and predictions

charts.title: Bayes' Theorem Components Summary
charts.content: Complete reference table defining each component of Bayes' Theorem with mathematical notation and interpretation. Includes: Posterior P(H|D) - updated probability of hypothesis after observing data; Likelihood P(D|H) - probability of data given hypothesis is true; Prior P(H) - initial belief before seeing data; Evidence P(D) - total probability of observing the data (normalizing constant). Each row includes formula, name, meaning, and role in Bayesian learning.

charts.title: Bayesian vs Frequentist Statistics: Key Differences
charts.content: Comparative analysis of two statistical paradigms covering: Nature of parameters (random vs fixed), Use of priors (yes vs no), Output interpretation (probability about parameters vs long-run frequency), Computational approach (integration vs optimization), Uncertainty quantification (full distribution vs intervals), and Example methods (Naïve Bayes, Bayesian Networks vs MLE, Hypothesis testing). Useful for exam preparation on differences between approaches.

charts.title: Naïve Bayes Classification Process Step-by-Step
charts.content: Decision tree showing classification workflow: 1) Input new instance with features, 2) Calculate prior probability P(Y) for each class from training data, 3) For each feature, calculate likelihood P(xᵢ|Y) assuming independence, 4) Compute unnormalized posterior: P(Y) × ∏ P(xᵢ|Y), 5) Calculate evidence P(X) as sum of all unnormalized posteriors, 6) Normalize posteriors by dividing by evidence, 7) Select class with maximum posterior probability, 8) Return predicted class label.

charts.title: Types of Naïve Bayes Classifiers
charts.content: Overview of three main Naïve Bayes variants: Gaussian Naïve Bayes - assumes continuous features follow normal distribution, used with continuous data like height, weight, temperature; Multinomial Naïve Bayes - works with discrete counts like word frequencies in text classification, spam detection; Bernoulli Naïve Bayes - binary/boolean features, presence/absence patterns, document classification. Includes use cases and assumptions for each type.

branches: Bayes' Theorem Foundation: Posterior = Likelihood × Prior / Evidence, Components: P(H|D), P(D|H), P(H), P(D), Mathematical basis for updating beliefs
Bayesian Learning Process: Observe Data → Calculate Likelihood → Update Prior to Posterior → Make Predictions → Repeat with new data, Iterative belief refinement

Naïve Bayes Classifier: Independence assumption P(X|Y) = ∏P(xᵢ|Y), Classify by maximizing posterior, Types: Gaussian, Multinomial, Bernoulli, Applications: Spam detection, sentiment analysis, medical diagnosis
Comparison with Frequentist: Parameters as random variables vs fixed constants, Uses prior distributions, Provides full probability distributions, More intuitive uncertainty quantification
Real-World Applications: Medical diagnosis with test results, Spam email filtering, Document classification, Weather prediction, Recommendation systems, Credit risk assessment
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all key concepts in Bayesian learning: from the foundational Bayes' Theorem through the practical Naïve Bayes classifier, comparison with frequentist methods, and applications. Helps students see the complete picture and understand relationships between theoretical foundations and practical implementations.
